content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 26, 2018

David W. Yam
46 Nace Avenue
Piedmont, CA 94611

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-17-292

Dear Mr. Yam:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that we provide advice under the post-government employment provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, Section 1090, or Public Contract Code Section 10411. Additionally, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

In anticipation of the ending of your retired annuitant Senior Landscape Architect position with the California Department of Transportation ("Caltrans"), are you prohibited under the "one year ban" or the "permanent ban" from the following:

(a) Consultant work with a private engineering/planning firm with similar duties as you currently perform relating to construction support and design service duties on the Doyle Drive Project?
(b) Consultant work with a private engineering/planning firm on transportation projects not related to the Doyle Drive Project?
(c) Employment with a County Public Transportation Agency?

CONCLUSION

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. A-17-292
Page No. 2

After leaving state employment, your future employment activities are restricted by the
permanent ban and one-year ban. Under the permanent ban, you may not, for compensation, make
an appearance or communication or aid, advise, counsel, consult, or assist in representing another
person (other than the State of California) before any state administrative agency regarding the
Doyle Drive Project permits or the implementation or monitoring of these permits. Under the one-
year ban, you may not appear before or communicate with CalTrans, for compensation, for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or property.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

You were employed as a Senior Landscape Architect with the California Department of
Transportation ("Caltrans") for 37 years, and retired on November 14, 2016. You returned to
Caltrans as a retired annuitant ("RA") on December 10, 2016, where you are currently employed.
As a Senior Landscape Architect, you supervised a staff and worked as a specialist on technical
design decisions, as well as formulating and implementing plans and procedures for comprehensive
landscape architecture, and land use planning projects. You also met and coordinated landscape
architectural projects with community, agency, and departmental leaders. You were a designated
employee at Caltrans and have regularly filed Statements of Economic Interest.

Prior to your retirement, you worked primarily on the Doyle Drive Project, a design/build
public and private partnership within the Presidio, and subject to the authority of the Presidio
Trust.³ Starting in 2008, you provided functional support related to landscape architectural/erosion
control/water quality work scope, environmental management and mitigation, as well as roadway
construction support/design services during various phases of this project. Your responsibilities
included working with Caltrans management, and field level construction and design support staff.

On the Doyle Drive Project, you also worked with the prime engineering contractors,
Flatiron/Kiewit Joint Venture and Golden Link Consortium, as well as representatives of the
Presidio Trust, the San Francisco Traffic Authority, regulatory agency managers and staff. Your
duties were to monitor the construction work, resolve field issues, and generally to represent the
State in a capacity to carry out completion of the project. Caltrans charged the engineering
contractors with obtaining the necessary permits from the Presidio Trust. You provided technical
and functional support. You also acted as a liaison for Caltrans to interface with the Presidio Trust
and the engineering contractors in securing the necessary permits. Once the permits were secured,
you acted as a liaison in resolving field issues with the permitted work while under construction.

Currently, you are working as an RA on the Doyle Drive Project construction close-out. In
addition to the duties outlined above, your duties include reviewing fencing, drainage, soil
placement, and storm water treatment plans. You review plans that may affect Caltrans facilities, permit
compliance related to the Regional Water Quality Control Board and the Department of Toxics and
Substance Control, as well as mitigation plans, planting plans, irrigation plans, contour grading
plans and soil management reports. This review involves assessing construction plans and reports
for accuracy, noting field changes and modifications ("as-built" condition) with written supportive

³ The Presidio Trust is a federal agency.

File No. A-17-292
Page No. 3

narrative, memorializing the finished condition at the close-out of a project, and compliance with
permits for close-out and notification to permitting agencies.

You state that in your work as an RA you do not have project decision-making authority,
nor do you "stamp" project plans as an architect. You are completing your RA period and wish to
possibly work for an engineering firm with an existing service contract on the Doyle Drive Project
to provide construction support/design services with duties similar to those you are performing as a
Caltrans RA.

ANALYSIS

Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban. (Sections 87400-
87406 and Regulation 18746.1.) In addition, an official who is in the process of leaving government
service may not make or use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision
directly relating to a business with which the official is negotiating prospective employment.
(Section 87407.) These provisions are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions.

Permanent Ban

The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and
participating for compensation in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other
specific parties, if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee participated while
employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.)

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any
oral or written communication made with the intent to influence a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while serving as a state administrative official. (Section
87401.) "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" means any "proceeding, application, request
for a ruling or other determination, contest, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state
administrative agency . . .." (Section 87400(c).)

An official or employee has "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the
proceeding "personally, and substantially" through "decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential
information . . .." (Section 87400(d).)

The permanent ban does not apply to "new" proceedings even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to, or grows out of, a prior proceeding in which the official had participated.
New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not participate
are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A "new" proceeding not
subject to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, a different subject matter, or
different factual issues from those considered in previous proceedings. (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-
04-187; also, see Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.)

File No. A-17-292
Page No. 4

The application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered a
proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or approval.
(Starovoytov Advice Letter, No. A-14-149, citing Anderson Advice Letter No. A-98-159, and
Blonien Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.) The "performance" or "implementation" proceeding is
narrowly construed and limited to the execution of the existing terms of an existing contract. (Lujan
Advice Letter, No. 14-009.) Where an employee has participated in the implementation proceeding,
the employee may not "switch sides" and work for compensation for an outside employer on the
same implementation proceeding. (Culp Advice Letter, No. I-14-051.)⁴

As a designated employee of Caltrans, you are subject to the permanent ban. To apply the
permanent ban to your situation, you must identify the proceedings in which you participated while
employed by the state. The permanent ban does not apply to projects in which you did not
participate.

Doyle Drive Project: You participated in permitting proceedings of the Doyle Drive Project.
You also participated in the monitoring and performance of permitted work on the Doyle Drive
Project. Your participation in each instance was "personal and substantial" in that it involved your
written recommendations, advice and approval. Accordingly, both proceedings are subject to the
permanent ban. Thus, once you leave state service you may not, for compensation, make an
appearance or communication or aid, advice, counsel, consult, or assist in representing another
person (other than the State of California) before a state administrative agency, or any officer or
employee thereof, regarding these Doyle Drive Project permits or the implementation or monitoring
of these permits.

Other Caltrans Projects: You indicate that you are interested in working for a private
engineer or a local government entity on projects that may or may not relate to Caltrans. You have
not provided any information as to your participation in any other specific proceeding as a Caltrans
employee that may affect your ability to engage in other areas. As a general rule, so long as your
work for another employer does not involve proceedings you participated in as an employee of
Caltrans, the permanent ban does not apply. Construction projects that are initiated after you leave
Caltrans, for example, are new proceedings to which the permanent ban does not apply, although
the one-year ban may restrict your activity for one year.

One-Year Ban

The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Section
87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date the
employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban applies
only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).)

___________________________

⁴ Culp Advice Letter No. I-14-051 previously superseded without notation the del Valle Advice Letter No. A-
12-086, as well as Guhl Advice Letter No. A-13-007; Johnson Advice Letter No. A-13-068; and Ogle Advice Letter
No. A-14-023 to the extent that each incorrectly advised that a former state employee who worked on the performance
proceeding may work on the same phase of the contract for a private contractor.

File No. A-17-292
Page No. 5

In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific parties such as "judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5).) An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Ibid.)

Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited, as applicable to your facts, if they are (1) before a state agency that the public official worked for or represented, or (2) before a state agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public official worked for or represented. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6).) There is an exception which allows the performance of services to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement to the extent that such services do not involve changes to any of these actions and the services are not prohibited under the permanent ban. (Regulation 18746.1(c).)

For purposes of the one-year ban, the date an official "permanently leaves state office or employment" is the date on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the duties of the position and stops performing the duties of the position. (Regulation 18746.4(b).) When an employee returns to state employment as a retired annuitant in a position that is designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code, the one-year ban begins anew, when the employee permanently leaves the retired annuitant position. (Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-14-184.)

The one year ban will apply to you, commencing at the end of your retired annuitant position. During the one-year ban period, you may not appear before or communicate with Caltrans, for compensation, for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative actions or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. You may, however, provide policy review and advice to a third-party contractor, if you are not identified with these efforts and so long as you are not otherwise prohibited from such representation under the permanent ban.

Negotiating Prospective Employment

A public official may negotiate and accept an offer of future employment before leaving his or her current state position. However, Section 87407 is designed to ensure that the official does not use his or her state position to make any decisions that unduly benefit the organization that is hiring the official. Section 87407 states:

No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, prospective employment.

File No. A-17-292
Page No. 6

Please note that this prohibition does not apply if the prospective employer is a state, local,
or federal governmental agency. (Regulation 18747(d)(3).)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

[signature]

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

related to the Doyle Drive Project? 
(c) Employment with a County Public Transportation Agency?

sections.analysis:
Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban. (Sections 87400-
87406 and Regulation 18746.1.) In addition, an official who is in the process ofleaving government 
service may not make or use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision 
directly relating to a business with which the official is negotiating prospective employment. 
(Section 87407. ) These provisions are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions. 
Permanent Ban 
. The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating for compensation in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee participated while 
employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1. ) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication made with the intent to influence a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceeding in which you participated while serving as a state administrative official. (Section 
87401.) "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" means any "proceeding, application, request 
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency . . .. " (Section 87400(c).) 
An official or employee has "participated" in a proceeding ifhe or she took part in the 
proceeding "personally, and substantially" through "decision, approval, disapproval, formal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential 
information . . .. " (Section 87400(d).) 
The permanent ban does not apply to "new" proceedings even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to, or grows out of, a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not participate 
are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. 1-89-649. ) A "new" proceeding not 
subject to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, a different subject matter, or 
different factual issues from those considered in previous proceedings. (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-
04-187; also, see Donovan Advice Letter, No. 1-03-119. ) 


PageNo.4 
The application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered a 
proceeding separate from the monitoring and perfonnance of the contract, license, or approval. 
(Starovoytov Advice Letter, No. A-14-149, citing Anderson Advice Letter No. A-98-159, and 
Blonien Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.) The "performance" or "implementation" proceeding is 
narrowly construed and limited to the execution of the existing tenns of an existing contract. (Lujan 
Advice Letter, No, 14-009.) Where an employee has participated in the implementation proceeding, 
the employee may not "switch sides" and work for compensation for an outside employer on the 
same implementation proceejing. ( Culp Advice Letter, No. 1-14-051.)4 
As a designated employee of Caltrans, you are subject to the permanent ban. To apply the 
permanent ban to your situation, you must identify the proceedings in which you participated while 
employed by the state. The permanent ban does not apply to projects in which you did not 
participate. 
Doyle Drive Project; You participated in permitting proceedings of the Doyle Drive Project. 
You also participated in the monitoring and perfonnance of permitted work on the Doyle Drive 
Project. Your participation in each instance was "personal and substantial" in that it involved your 
written recommendations, advice and approval. Accordingly, both proceedings are subject to the 
pennanent ban. Thus, once you leave state service you may not, for compensation, make an 
appearance or communication or aid, advice, counsel, consult, or assist in representing another 
person (other than the State of California) before a state administrative agency, or any officer or 
employee thereof, regarding these Doyle Drive Project permits or the implementation or monitoring 
of these permits. 
Other Caltrans Projects: You indicate that you are interested in working for a private 
engineer or a local government entity on projects that may or may not relate to Caltrans. You have 
not provided any information as to your participation in any other specific proceeding as a Caltrans 
employee that may affect your ability to engage in other areas. As a general rule, so long as your 
work for another employer does not involve proceedings you participated in as an employee of 
Cal trans, the permanent ban does not apply. Construction projects that are initiated after you leave 
Caltrans, for example, are new proceedings to which the permanent ban does not apply, although 
the one-year ban may restrict your activity for one year. 
One-Year Ban 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Section 
87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746. l(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date the 
employee pennanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban applies 
only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or 
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.I(b)(3) and (4).) 
4 Culp Advice Letter No. 1-14-051 previously superseded without notation the def Valle Advice Letter No. A-
12-086, as well as Guhl Advice Letter No. A-13-007; Johnson Advice Letter No. A-13-068; and Ogle Advice Letter 
No. A-14-023 to the extent that each incorrectly advised that a fonner state employee who worked on the perfonnance 
proceeding may work on the same phase of the contract for a private contractor.  
In contrast to the pennanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific 
parties such as ')udicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance 
or communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or 
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation 
of a pennit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 
18746.l(b)(S).) An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" ifit is made 
for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 187 46.2.) An appearance or communication 
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by 
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Ibid.) 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited, as applicable to your facts, if they 
are (1) before a state agency that the public official worked for or represented, or (2) before a state 
agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency 
the public official worked for or represented. (Regulation 18746.1 (b)(6).) There is an exception 
which allows the perfonnance of services to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an 
existing pennit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement to the extent that such services do not 
involve changes to any of these actions and the services are not prohibited under the pennanent ban. 
(Regulation 18746. l(c).) 
For purposes of the one-year ban, the date an official "pennanently leaves state office or 
employment" is the date on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the duties of the 
position and stops performing the duties of the position. (Regulation 18746.4(b).) When an 
employee returns to state employment as a retired annuitant in a position that is designated in the 
agency's conflict-of-interest code, the one-year ban begins anew, when the employee pennanently 
leaves the retired annuitant position. (Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-14-184.) 
The one year ban will apply to you, commencing at the end of your retired annuitant 
position. During the one-year ban period, you may not appear before or communicate with Caltrans, 
for compensation, for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative actions or influencing 
any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, 
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. You may, however, provide 
policy review and advice to a third-party contractor, if you are not identified with these efforts and 
so long as you are not otherwise prohibited from such representation under the permanent ban. 
Negotiating Prospective Employment 
A public official may negotiate and accept an offer of future employment before leaving his 
or her current state position. However, Section 87407 is designed to ensure that the official does not 
use his or her state position to make any decisions that unduly benefit the organization that is hiring 
the official. Section 87407 states: 
No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official 
position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person 
with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, 
prospective employment.  
Please note that this prohibition does not apply if the prospective employer is a state, local, 
or federal governmental agency. (Regulation 18747(d)(3).)

for accuracy, noting field changes and modifications ("as-built" condition) with written supportive 
3 The Presidio Trust is a federal agency.  
narrative, memorializing the finished condition at the close-out of a project, and compliance with 
permits for close-out and notification to permitting agencies. 
You state that in your work as an RA you do not have project decision-making authority, 
nor do you "stamp" project plans as an architect. You are completing your RA period and wish to 
possibly work for an engineering firm with an existing service contract on the Doyle Drive Project 
to provide construction support/design services with duties similar to those you are performing as a 
Caltrans RA.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 February 26, 2018 David W. Yam 46 Nace Avenue Piedmont, CA 94611 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. I-17-292 Dear Mr. Yam: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that we provide advice under the post-government employment provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, Section 1090, or Public Contract Code Section 10411. Additionally, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. QUESTION In anticipation of the ending of your retired annuitant Senior Landscape Architect position with the California Department of Transportation ("Caltrans"), are you prohibited under the "one year ban" or the "permanent ban" from the following: (a) Consultant work with a private engineering/planning firm with similar duties as you currently perform relating to construction support and design service duties on the Doyle Drive Project? (b) Consultant work with a private engineering/planning firm on transportation projects not related to the Doyle Drive Project? (c) Employment with a County Public Transportation Agency? CONCLUSION ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. A-17-292 Page No. 2 After leaving state employment, your future employment activities are restricted by the permanent ban and one-year ban. Under the permanent ban, you may not, for compensation, make an appearance or communication or aid, advise, counsel, consult, or assist in representing another person (other than the State of California) before any state administrative agency regarding the Doyle Drive Project permits or the implementation or monitoring of these permits. Under the one- year ban, you may not appear before or communicate with CalTrans, for compensation, for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR You were employed as a Senior Landscape Architect with the California Department of Transportation ("Caltrans") for 37 years, and retired on November 14, 2016. You returned to Caltrans as a retired annuitant ("RA") on
